sources.sourceName: OpenStax Psychology 2e, Ch. 5 Review Questions (Sensation and Perception) and Ch. 6 Review Questions (Learning)

sources.sourceName: OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (answer key for odd-numbered review questions)

meta.notes: Psychology (PSBB) items scraped verbatim from OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch. 5 and Ch. 6 review-questions pages; correct answers from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), odd-numbered review questions only. Live OpenStax pages show CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 plus a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing/non-AI per PRD.

hints.prompt: This question is about psychophysics, the study of how physical stimuli relate to what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Our psychological sensations and perceptions of those stimuli

hints.rationale: Psychophysics links physical stimuli to the sensations they produce.

hints.prompt: The difference threshold (just noticeable difference) measures which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The smallest CHANGE between two stimuli that a person can detect

choices.label: B
choices.text: The weakest single stimulus detectable half the time

choices.label: C
choices.text: The conversion of energy into neural signals

choices.label: D
choices.text: The loudest sound a person can tolerate

hints.rationale: The difference threshold is about detecting a change, not the minimum detectable stimulus, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because both refer to detecting a CHANGE between stimuli rather than detecting a single faint stimulus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: difference threshold and just noticeable difference

hints.rationale: The difference threshold and the just noticeable difference are the same change-detection concept, so both can be eliminated.

questions.stem: ________ refers to the minimum amount of stimulus energy required to be detected 50% of the time.
questions.explanation: The absolute threshold is the minimum stimulus energy needed for a person to detect a stimulus 50% of the time.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 5 Q1 = A.

hints.prompt: The four options are all terms drawn from the study of which topic?
hints.rationale: All four are sensation-and-perception terms.

hints.prompt: Sensory adaptation refers to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduced sensitivity to a constant, unchanging stimulus over time

choices.label: B
choices.text: The conversion of stimulus energy into neural impulses

choices.label: C
choices.text: Failing to notice an unexpected object when attention is elsewhere

choices.label: D
choices.text: The smallest detectable difference between two stimuli

hints.rationale: Sensory adaptation is decreased responsiveness to a constant stimulus, not energy conversion, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one is an attention failure (missing an unexpected object) and the other concerns detecting differences between stimuli - neither being energy conversion?
hints.rationale: Inattentional blindness is an attention phenomenon and the difference threshold is about detecting change, so neither converts energy.

questions.stem: ________ involves the conversion of sensory stimulus energy into neural impulses.
questions.explanation: Transduction is the conversion of physical sensory stimulus energy into the electrochemical neural impulses the nervous system can process.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 5 Q3 = D.

hints.prompt: In the visible spectrum, the color we perceive is determined mainly by which physical property of light?
hints.rationale: Color perception depends on the wavelength of light.

hints.prompt: Among red, orange, and yellow, which color has the LONGEST wavelength?
hints.rationale: Red has the longest wavelength of the three, so a correct short-to-long ordering must end in red.

hints.prompt: Among red, orange, and yellow, which has the SHORTEST wavelength, so it would come FIRST when ordering from short to long?

choices.label: D
choices.text: None of them has the shortest

hints.rationale: Yellow has the shortest wavelength, so a correct short-to-long ordering must begin with yellow, eliminating any sequence that does not.

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly matches the pattern in our perception of color as we move from short wavelengths to long wavelengths?
questions.explanation: Wavelength increases from yellow (~570 nm) to orange (~600 nm) to red (~700 nm), so moving from shorter to longer wavelengths gives yellow to orange to red.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 5 Q5 = B.

hints.prompt: The electromagnetic spectrum is the full range of what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Electromagnetic radiation across all wavelengths and frequencies

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sound waves audible to the human ear

choices.label: D
choices.text: The types of neurons in the brain

hints.rationale: The electromagnetic spectrum spans all electromagnetic radiation.

hints.prompt: About how much of the entire electromagnetic spectrum is visible light?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Only a small portion; most electromagnetic radiation is invisible to us

choices.label: B
choices.text: The entire spectrum is visible light

choices.label: C
choices.text: None of the spectrum is visible light

hints.rationale: Visible light is only a tiny slice; much of the spectrum is invisible to the human eye.

hints.prompt: Infrared light is best classified as which of the following?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A type of mechanical sound wave

choices.label: C
choices.text: A chemical element on the periodic table

hints.rationale: Infrared is one (invisible) form of electromagnetic radiation, reinforcing that the spectrum includes more than visible light.

questions.stem: The electromagnetic spectrum includes ________.
questions.explanation: The electromagnetic spectrum spans radio waves, infrared, visible light, ultraviolet, x-rays, and more, so all of the listed options are included.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 5 Q7 = D.

hints.prompt: Which of the following is a physical property of a sound wave?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Its frequency (how many cycles occur per second)

choices.label: C
choices.text: The color of the object making the sound

choices.label: D
choices.text: The body weight of the listener

hints.rationale: Frequency is a core physical property of a sound wave.

hints.prompt: Pitch is the perceptual quality determined mainly by which single property of a sound wave?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Its combination of frequency, amplitude, and timing

hints.rationale: Pitch depends mainly on frequency alone, not the combination of all three, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated - one because it depends on frequency alone, the other because it wrongly labels sound as an electromagnetic (rather than mechanical) wave?
hints.rationale: Pitch is set by frequency alone and 'electromagnetic' misclassifies sound (a mechanical wave), so both can be eliminated.

questions.stem: The quality of a sound that is affected by frequency, amplitude, and timing of the sound wave is known as ________.
questions.explanation: Timbre is the quality or 'color' of a sound that lets us distinguish sources; it depends on the combination of frequency, amplitude, and timing of the sound wave.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 5 Q9 = D.

hints.prompt: The retina contains two types of photoreceptors. What do these light-detecting cells do?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Convert light into neural signals for vision

hints.rationale: Photoreceptors transduce light into neural signals.

hints.prompt: Rods, one type of photoreceptor, function best under which conditions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dim, low-light conditions, and they do not detect color

choices.label: B
choices.text: Bright light, and they capture fine color detail

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only in total darkness with no light present

hints.rationale: Rods work best in dim light and are color-blind, so they are not the bright-light receptor.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one carries visual output out of the eye and the other is a brain region for processing vision - neither being a light-detecting photoreceptor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: retinal ganglion cells and striate cortex

hints.rationale: Retinal ganglion cells transmit signals out of the eye and the striate cortex is a visual brain area, so neither is a photoreceptor.

questions.stem: ________ operate best under bright light conditions.
questions.explanation: Cones are the photoreceptors specialized for color and fine detail and operate best under bright (photopic) light; rods handle dim light.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 5 Q11 = A.

hints.prompt: A negative afterimage (seeing a color after staring at another) is best explained by which theory of color vision?
hints.rationale: Afterimages are explained by the opponent-process theory of color vision.

hints.prompt: In opponent-process theory, which of these is a genuine opposing color pair?
hints.rationale: Blue-yellow is one of the opponent color pairs (red-green is the other).

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because blue and yellow are each other's opponent colors, so neither is the opponent of green?
hints.rationale: Blue and yellow form their own opponent pair, so neither is green's opponent color.

questions.stem: If you were to stare at a green dot for a relatively long period of time and then shift your gaze to a blank white screen, you would see a ________ negative afterimage.
questions.explanation: Opponent-process theory predicts that fatiguing the green channel produces a red negative afterimage, because red and green are opponent colors.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 5 Q13 = D.

hints.prompt: The three tiny bones (ossicles) of the middle ear serve what function?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transmitting and amplifying sound vibrations toward the inner ear

hints.rationale: The middle-ear ossicles transmit and amplify sound vibrations.

hints.prompt: The pinna is which part of the ear?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The visible outer ear, not a middle-ear bone

choices.label: B
choices.text: One of the three middle-ear ossicles

choices.label: C
choices.text: A fluid-filled organ of the inner ear

choices.label: D
choices.text: The nerve that carries sound to the brain

hints.rationale: The pinna is the outer ear, so any set of structures listing it is not the middle-ear ossicles.

hints.prompt: The cochlea and pinna are inner- and outer-ear structures, so a set listing them is not the ossicles. Which structure below IS one of the three middle-ear ossicles?
hints.rationale: The stapes is a middle-ear ossicle, whereas the cochlea and pinna are not, so a set naming them can be eliminated.

questions.stem: The three ossicles of the middle ear are known as ________.
questions.explanation: The three middle-ear ossicles are the malleus, incus, and stapes, also known by their common names hammer, anvil, and stirrup, so both A and B are correct.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 5 Q15 = D.

hints.prompt: In sensory terms, the prefixes 'mono-' and 'bi-/bin-' indicate what, respectively?
hints.rationale: 'Mono-' means one and 'bi-/bin-' means two.

hints.prompt: In these terms, the root '-aural' refers to hearing or ears, while the root '-ocular' refers to what?
hints.rationale: '-ocular' refers to the eyes, so eye-based terms are not ear cues.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because both refer to VISION (the eyes) rather than to hearing?
hints.rationale: Monocular and binocular are vision terms, not hearing cues, so both can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Cues that require two ears are referred to as ________ cues.
questions.explanation: Binaural cues are localization cues that require both ears (for example, interaural time and intensity differences). Monaural cues require only one ear.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 5 Q17 = D.

hints.prompt: How many basic taste qualities does the human tongue detect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Five (sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and a savory taste)

choices.label: D
choices.text: None; taste is entirely a matter of smell

hints.rationale: There are five basic tastes, one of which MSG is linked to.

hints.prompt: Monosodium glutamate is added to food to enhance which sensory quality?
hints.rationale: MSG enhances a savory flavor, not sweetness or sourness.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one is the taste of sugars and the other is the taste often linked to coffee, dark chocolate, or potential toxins?
hints.rationale: Sweet is the taste of sugars and bitter the taste of things like coffee or toxins, so neither is MSG's savory taste.

questions.stem: Which taste is associated with monosodium glutamate?
questions.explanation: Monosodium glutamate (MSG) elicits the savory taste known as umami, one of the five basic tastes.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 5 Q19 = C.

hints.prompt: Maintaining balance and body posture depends on a sense that detects what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Head position, movement, and the pull of gravity

hints.rationale: Balance depends on sensing head position, movement, and gravity.

hints.prompt: The auditory nerve carries which type of information to the brain?
hints.rationale: The auditory nerve carries hearing signals, not balance information, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one detects pain and the other processes smell - neither maintaining balance?
hints.rationale: Nociceptors sense pain and the olfactory bulb handles smell, so neither maintains balance.

questions.stem: Which of the following is involved in maintaining balance and body posture?
questions.explanation: The vestibular system of the inner ear senses head position and movement and is essential for balance and body posture.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 5 Q21 = D.

hints.prompt: The four options (closure, good continuation, proximity, similarity) are all examples of which broad set of principles?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The Gestalt principles of perceptual organization

hints.rationale: All four are Gestalt grouping principles.

hints.prompt: The Gestalt principle of proximity states that we group elements based on what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: How close together they are in space

choices.label: B
choices.text: How we mentally fill in missing pieces to see a whole

choices.label: C
choices.text: How similar they look to one another

choices.label: D
choices.text: How they move together in the same direction

hints.rationale: Proximity groups items by nearness, not by completing figures, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one groups items by how alike they look and the other by following smooth, continuous lines - neither being about completing a whole figure?
hints.rationale: Similarity groups by likeness and good continuation follows continuous paths, so neither completes a whole figure.

questions.stem: Our tendency to perceive things as complete objects rather than as a series of parts is known as the principle of ________.
questions.explanation: The Gestalt principle of closure is our tendency to fill in gaps and perceive incomplete figures as complete, whole objects.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 5 Q23 = A.

hints.prompt: When you look at a scene, your visual system separates it into two parts. What are these two parts?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A main object of focus and its background

choices.label: B
choices.text: A near object and a far object

choices.label: C
choices.text: A bright color and a dark color

hints.rationale: Figure-ground organization separates the focal object from its background.

hints.prompt: In figure-ground organization, the 'ground' refers to which part?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The background behind the main object

choices.label: B
choices.text: The main object of focus itself

choices.label: C
choices.text: The tendency to fill in gaps to see a whole

choices.label: D
choices.text: An expectation shaped by prior experience

hints.rationale: The 'ground' is the background, not the focal object, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one is a gap-filling grouping principle and the other is a mental expectation or bias - neither naming the focal object of a scene?
hints.rationale: Closure is a grouping principle and perceptual set is an expectation, so neither is the 'main point of focus.'

questions.stem: The main point of focus in a visual display is known as the ________.
questions.explanation: In figure-ground organization, the figure is the main object of focus that stands out from the background (ground).
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 5 Q25 = D.

hints.prompt: A reflex is best defined as which kind of response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An unlearned, automatic response to a stimulus

choices.label: B
choices.text: A skill acquired through years of practice

choices.label: D
choices.text: A social custom taught by parents

hints.rationale: A reflex is an unlearned, automatic response.

hints.prompt: Riding a bike and walking are best described as which type of behavior?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Learned motor skills acquired through practice and development

choices.label: B
choices.text: Innate reflexes present without any learning

hints.rationale: Biking and walking are learned motor skills, not reflexes, so those options can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because both are learned or socialized behaviors rather than automatic, unlearned reflexes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: a teen socializing and a child riding a bike

choices.label: B
choices.text: an infant sucking on a nipple and a teen socializing

choices.label: C
choices.text: a toddler walking and an infant sucking on a nipple

choices.label: D
choices.text: a child riding a bike and an infant sucking on a nipple

hints.rationale: Socializing and bike-riding are learned behaviors, not reflexes, so both can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a reflex that occurs at some point in the development of a human being?
questions.explanation: A reflex is an unlearned, automatic response to a stimulus. An infant sucking on a nipple is a reflex, whereas the other options are learned behaviors.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 6 Q1 = C.

hints.prompt: Associative learning is learning that involves what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Forming connections between stimuli, or between a behavior and its consequences

choices.label: C
choices.text: The physical growth of the body over time

hints.rationale: Associative learning is about forming associations between events.

hints.prompt: Classical conditioning is also known by which other name?
hints.rationale: Classical and Pavlovian conditioning are two names for the same process, so listing both as 'two forms' is redundant.

hints.prompt: Which of these is NOT one of the two classic forms of associative learning?
hints.rationale: Observational learning is a separate (non-associative) type, so any pairing that includes it, or that uses the made-up term 'learning conditioning,' can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Two forms of associative learning are ________ and ________.
questions.explanation: The two major forms of associative learning are classical conditioning and operant conditioning. (Classical and Pavlovian conditioning are the same thing.)
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 6 Q3 = A.

hints.prompt: In classical conditioning, a 'stimulus' is which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Something in the environment that can trigger, or come to trigger, a response

choices.label: B
choices.text: The learner's internal level of motivation

choices.label: C
choices.text: A reward delivered after a behavior

choices.label: D
choices.text: The total number of learning trials

hints.rationale: A stimulus is an environmental event that can trigger a response.

hints.prompt: An unconditioned stimulus (US) is defined by which feature?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It naturally and automatically triggers a response without any learning

choices.label: B
choices.text: It produces no response at all when first presented

choices.label: C
choices.text: It works only after a period of conditioning

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is a type of reinforcement schedule

hints.rationale: A US automatically triggers a response, the opposite of producing no initial response, so it can be ruled out.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one triggers a response only AFTER learning and the other is a RESPONSE rather than a stimulus?
hints.rationale: A conditioned stimulus elicits a response only after learning and an unconditioned response is a response, not a stimulus, so neither fits.

questions.stem: A stimulus that does not initially elicit a response in an organism is a(n) ________.
questions.explanation: A neutral stimulus initially produces no relevant response; through classical conditioning it can become a conditioned stimulus.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 6 Q5 = B.

hints.prompt: In classical conditioning, extinction refers to what happening to a learned response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The conditioned response gradually weakens and disappears

choices.label: C
choices.text: A brand-new inborn reflex is created

hints.rationale: Extinction is the weakening and disappearance of the conditioned response.

hints.prompt: By the time extinction occurs, the once-neutral stimulus has already become which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A conditioned stimulus that now triggers the learned response

choices.label: B
choices.text: Still just a neutral stimulus with no effect

hints.rationale: After conditioning, the neutral stimulus has become a conditioned stimulus, so extinction involves the conditioned (not 'neutral') stimulus.

hints.prompt: Which two of the main answer choices can be eliminated because they describe a 'neutral stimulus' that has actually already become a conditioned stimulus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the two options that begin 'the neutral stimulus is presented repeatedly'

choices.label: B
choices.text: the two options that begin 'the conditioned stimulus'

choices.label: C
choices.text: the options that mention an unconditioned stimulus

choices.label: D
choices.text: none of them can be eliminated

hints.rationale: By the time extinction happens the stimulus is conditioned, not neutral, so both 'neutral stimulus' options can be eliminated.

questions.stem: Extinction occurs when ________.

choices.label: A
choices.text: the conditioned stimulus is presented repeatedly without being paired with an unconditioned stimulus

choices.label: B
choices.text: the unconditioned stimulus is presented repeatedly without being paired with a conditioned stimulus

choices.label: C
choices.text: the neutral stimulus is presented repeatedly without being paired with an unconditioned stimulus

choices.label: D
choices.text: the neutral stimulus is presented repeatedly without being paired with a conditioned stimulus

questions.explanation: In classical conditioning, extinction is the weakening of the conditioned response when the conditioned stimulus is repeatedly presented without the unconditioned stimulus.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 6 Q7 = A.

hints.prompt: In operant conditioning, what is the difference between 'reinforcement' and 'punishment'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reinforcement increases a behavior; punishment decreases it

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reinforcement decreases a behavior; punishment increases it

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both of them increase a behavior

choices.label: D
choices.text: Neither has any effect on behavior

hints.rationale: Reinforcement increases behavior while punishment decreases it.

hints.prompt: In these operant terms, what do the words 'positive' and 'negative' mean?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Positive means adding a stimulus; negative means removing a stimulus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Positive means good; negative means bad

choices.label: C
choices.text: Positive means fast; negative means slow

choices.label: D
choices.text: Positive means reward; negative means punishment

hints.rationale: 'Positive' means adding and 'negative' means removing a stimulus, not good versus bad.

hints.prompt: Since the goal here is to STOP (decrease) a behavior, which two options can be eliminated because they would INCREASE a behavior instead?
hints.rationale: Both forms of reinforcement increase behavior, so they cannot be used to stop one.

questions.stem: ________ is when you take away a pleasant stimulus to stop a behavior.
questions.explanation: Negative punishment removes a pleasant stimulus to decrease a behavior (for example, taking away a privilege). 'Negative' means something is removed; 'punishment' means the behavior decreases.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 6 Q9 = D.

hints.prompt: 'Successive approximations' means rewarding behaviors that do what over time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Get progressively closer to a desired target behavior

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stay exactly identical on every trial

hints.rationale: Successive approximations are steps that move progressively closer to a goal behavior.

hints.prompt: How does this process differ from simply delivering positive reinforcement?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It reinforces a SEQUENCE of gradually improving approximations, not just one fixed behavior

choices.label: B
choices.text: It removes an unpleasant stimulus each time

choices.label: C
choices.text: It weakens a behavior until it disappears

choices.label: D
choices.text: It involves no rewards at all

hints.rationale: This technique reinforces gradually improving steps, which is more specific than one-off reinforcement.

hints.prompt: Which pair can be eliminated because one WEAKENS a behavior by withholding reinforcement and the other works by REMOVING an unpleasant stimulus - neither being 'rewarding step-by-step progress'?
hints.rationale: Extinction weakens behavior and negative reinforcement removes an aversive stimulus, so neither is rewarding successive approximations.

questions.stem: Rewarding successive approximations toward a target behavior is ________.
questions.explanation: Shaping is reinforcing successive approximations, that is, rewarding behaviors that get progressively closer to a desired target behavior.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 6 Q11 = A.

hints.prompt: Learning by watching and imitating someone else is called what?
hints.rationale: Learning by watching and imitating is observational learning, or modeling.

hints.prompt: In observational learning, the demonstrator is referred to by a technical term regardless of whether they intend to teach. This means the demonstrator could even be which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Someone who is not a formal teacher at all, such as a peer or a TV character

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only one of the learner's parents

hints.rationale: The demonstrator need not be a formal instructor, so job-title options are too narrow.

hints.prompt: Which two options are job titles that specifically imply athletic or classroom instruction, making them narrower than the general term for any demonstrator?
hints.rationale: 'Instructor' and 'coach' denote specific instructing roles, narrower than the general term for any demonstrator.

questions.stem: The person who performs a behavior that serves as an example is called a ________.
questions.explanation: In observational learning, the individual who performs the behavior that is observed and imitated is the model.
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax Psychology 2e Student Solutions Guide (CC BY 4.0), Ch. 6 Q13 = B.

hints.prompt: The four steps of observational learning (modeling) were proposed by which theorist?
hints.rationale: Bandura proposed the four-step model of observational learning.

hints.prompt: Logically, before you can imitate a behavior, what must you do first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pay attention to the behavior being modeled

choices.label: B
choices.text: Feel motivated by a reward received afterward

choices.label: D
choices.text: Forget the details of the behavior

hints.rationale: You must attend to a behavior first, so the correct sequence must begin with attention.

hints.prompt: Which two orderings can be eliminated because they incorrectly place 'motivation' as the very FIRST step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: the two sequences that begin with 'motivation'

choices.label: B
choices.text: the two sequences that begin with 'attention'

choices.label: D
choices.text: none of them can be eliminated

hints.rationale: Attention must come before motivation, so any sequence starting with motivation is wrong.

questions.stem: Which is the correct order of steps in the modeling process?
questions.explanation: Bandura's four steps of observational learning (modeling) occur in the order attention, retention, reproduction, and motivation.
